definition: A firm statement meaning the speaker will not tolerate or accept a particular behavior or situation.
examples: Shouting at your sister like that — I won't have it in this house!
He said he won't have any more excuses for being late.
memoryTrick: I WON'T HAVE IT means refusing to "hold" or accept a behavior any longer — a firm boundary being set.
commonMistake: Confusing "I won't have it" (I won't tolerate it) with the literal meaning "I will not possess it" — context makes clear it's about tolerance, not ownership.

definition: To let someone down or disappoint them, especially by not meeting their trust or expectations.
examples: I feel like I failed you when you needed me most.
He promised never to fail his team again after the missed deadline.
memoryTrick: TO FAIL SOMEONE means you didn't come through for them, breaking their trust or expectation.
commonMistake: Confusing "fail someone" (let them down emotionally) with "fail an exam" (not pass a test) — the same verb, very different objects and implications.

definition: To solemnly renounce or give up a belief, claim, or practice.
examples: He publicly abjured his former political beliefs.
She abjured all claims to the family estate.
memoryTrick: ABJURE sounds like "a big JURY" swearing you away from something forever.
commonMistake: Confusing "abjure" (to renounce) with "adjure" (to urge solemnly) — nearly identical spelling, opposite direction of action.

definition: To urge or request someone solemnly or earnestly to do something.
examples: The elder adjured the young men to keep their promise.
She adjured her colleagues to reconsider the decision.
memoryTrick: ADJURE = AD (toward) + JURE (swear) — swearing someone toward doing something.
commonMistake: Confusing "adjure" (to urge earnestly) with "abjure" (to renounce) — easy to mix up since only one letter differs.

definition: To plant seeds in the ground so that they will grow.
examples: Farmers sow wheat in the early spring.
The rumor was designed to sow discord among the team.
memoryTrick: SOW rhymes with "go" not "cow" — think of scattering seeds as you go along a field.
commonMistake: Confusing "sow" (to plant seeds, pronounced like "so") with "sow" (a female pig, pronounced like "cow") — same spelling, different pronunciation and meaning.

definition: To gather a crop as it becomes ready, typically at the end of a growing season.
examples: The farmers harvested the wheat before the storm arrived.
This year's harvest was the best in a decade.
memoryTrick: HARVEST relates to "autumn" — traditionally the season when crops are gathered.
commonMistake: Confusing "harvest" (to gather crops) with "harness" (to control or use something, like energy) — similar sound, different meaning.

definition: Kindness and goodwill; a disposition to do good and be generous toward others.
examples: The charity relies on the benevolence of local donors.
Her benevolence toward strangers was well known in the village.
memoryTrick: BENEVOLENCE = BENE (well) + VOL (wish) — wishing others well.
commonMistake: Confusing "benevolence" (kindness) with "benevolent" (the adjective form) — remember benevolence is the noun, describing the quality itself.

definition: To formally put an end to a law, system, or practice; to nullify or cancel officially.
examples: The government voted to abolish the outdated tax law.
Slavery was formally abolished in many countries in the 19th century.
memoryTrick: ABOLISH sounds like "a-POLISH" it away — wiping something out completely.
commonMistake: Confusing "abolish" (end a law/system permanently) with "abandon" (give up on something, often temporarily or informally) — abolish is a formal, permanent legal action.

definition: To include something as part of a whole; to combine or integrate.
examples: The final design incorporates feedback from all the reviewers.
The company was incorporated in 1998.
memoryTrick: INCORPORATE = IN (into) + CORPOR (body) — bringing something into the body of a whole.
commonMistake: Confusing "incorporate" (to include as part of something) with "corporate" (relating to a large company) — same root, different word class and meaning.

definition: To delay or prevent something by obstructing it; to hinder progress.
rootOrigin: Latin "impedire" (in- "in" + pes "foot", literally to entangle the feet)
examples: Heavy snow impeded traffic across the entire region.
Lack of funding continues to impede the research project.
memoryTrick: IMPEDE relates to "feet" (pes) — literally tangling someone's feet so they can't move forward.
commonMistake: Confusing "impede" (to hinder/slow down) with "impel" (to drive or urge forward) — near-opposite meanings, similar spelling.

definition: Too powerful to be defeated or overcome; unconquerable.
examples: The champion seemed invincible until his shocking loss last night.
For years, the empire's army was considered invincible.
memoryTrick: INVINCIBLE = IN (not) + VINC (conquer) — impossible to conquer.
commonMistake: Confusing "invincible" (cannot be defeated) with "invisible" (cannot be seen) — similar spelling, completely different meaning.

definition: To wait somewhere secretly, especially with the intention to attack, or to exist unnoticed.
rootOrigin: Middle English, possibly from Scandinavian origin related to "lower" (to scowl)
examples: A predator lurked in the bushes, waiting for its prey.
Doubts still lurked in the back of her mind.
memoryTrick: LURK sounds sneaky — like something hiding quietly in the dark, waiting.
commonMistake: Confusing "lurk" (to hide and wait) with "lark" (a bird, or a fun adventure) — similar sound, unrelated meaning.

definition: Physical strength and good health; energy and enthusiasm.
examples: She approached the new project with great vigor.
Despite his age, he still works with remarkable vigor.
memoryTrick: VIGOR relates to "vigorous" — think of someone bursting with lively energy.
commonMistake: Confusing "vigor" (energy/strength) with "rigor" (strictness/thoroughness) — similar sound, different meaning.

definition: To encourage or arouse interest, activity, or growth in something or someone.
examples: Lower interest rates are meant to stimulate economic growth.
The lecture stimulated a lively discussion among students.
memoryTrick: STIMULATE relates to a "stimulus" — something that prods or pokes a reaction into being.
commonMistake: Confusing "stimulate" (to encourage activity) with "simulate" (to imitate or pretend) — similar spelling, unrelated meaning.

definition: Understood by very few; mysterious or secret, especially relating to specialized or esoteric knowledge.
examples: The tax code is full of arcane rules that few people understand.
He was fascinated by arcane rituals from ancient cultures.
memoryTrick: ARCANE relates to "ark" or "chest" — knowledge locked away and hidden from most people.
commonMistake: Confusing "arcane" (mysterious, obscure knowledge) with "archaic" (old-fashioned, outdated) — related in feel but describe different qualities.

definition: Very old or old-fashioned; no longer in ordinary use.
examples: The contract contained archaic legal language few could understand.
Some of the company's archaic policies are finally being updated.
memoryTrick: ARCHAIC relates to "archaeology" — the study of very old, ancient things.
commonMistake: Confusing "archaic" (old-fashioned/outdated) with "arcane" (mysterious/secret) — related in feel but not synonyms.

definition: In a state of nervous excitement, anxiety, or distress; also, excessively elaborate or overdone.
examples: She became overwrought after hearing the unexpected news.
Critics found the film's ending overwrought and melodramatic.
memoryTrick: OVERWROUGHT = OVER + WROUGHT (worked) — worked up way too much, emotionally or stylistically.
commonMistake: Confusing "overwrought" (excessively emotional or elaborate) with "overworked" (having too much work) — related in form but different meanings.

definition: To make something by combining various ingredients or elements, especially something devious or creative.
examples: He concocted an elaborate excuse for being late.
The chef concocted a unique dish using leftover ingredients.
memoryTrick: CONCOCT relates to "cook together" — mixing ingredients (or ideas) to create something new.
commonMistake: Confusing "concoct" (to devise/mix together) with "conduct" (to lead or carry out) — similar sound, different meaning.

definition: To officially forbid or prohibit something, especially by law or custom.
examples: The use of certain chemicals is proscribed by international law.
The regime proscribed any form of public protest.
memoryTrick: PROSCRIBE sounds like "prescribe" but means the opposite — think PRO (against) + SCRIBE (write it down as forbidden).
commonMistake: Confusing "proscribe" (to forbid) with "prescribe" (to recommend or authorize) — a classic pair of near-opposite look-alikes.

definition: To recommend or authorize the use of something, especially medicine, or to lay down a rule authoritatively.
examples: The doctor prescribed antibiotics for the infection.
The manual prescribes a specific procedure for handling complaints.
memoryTrick: PRESCRIBE = PRE (before) + SCRIBE (write) — writing down instructions in advance for someone to follow.
commonMistake: Confusing "prescribe" (to recommend/authorize) with "proscribe" (to forbid) — opposite meanings despite near-identical spelling.

definition: To attribute something to a cause, quality, or source; to credit or assign responsibility.
examples: She ascribed her success to years of hard work and discipline.
The ancient Greeks ascribed natural disasters to the anger of the gods.
memoryTrick: ASCRIBE = A (to) + SCRIBE (write) — writing/attaching a cause or quality onto something.
commonMistake: Confusing "ascribe" (to attribute a cause) with "subscribe" (to sign up for something) — same root "scribe" but very different usage.

definition: Unable to be solved or explained; also used to describe a substance that cannot be dissolved in a liquid.
examples: The two sides reached what seemed like an insoluble dispute.
Certain minerals are insoluble in water.
memoryTrick: INSOLUBLE = IN (not) + SOLUBLE (able to be solved/dissolved) — impossible to resolve or dissolve.
commonMistake: Confusing "insoluble" (cannot be solved/dissolved) with "insolvent" (unable to pay debts) — similar root and spelling, unrelated modern meaning.

definition: To happen afterward or as a result of a previous event; to follow.
examples: A heated argument ensued after the referee's controversial call.
In the ensuing chaos, several documents were lost.
memoryTrick: ENSUE sounds like "in-SUE" — one event follows and pursues another, like a chase.
commonMistake: Confusing "ensue" (to follow/result from) with "ensure" (to make certain) — similar sound, different meaning.

definition: The period or consequences following a significant, often unpleasant, event.
examples: The city struggled to rebuild in the aftermath of the earthquake.
In the aftermath of the scandal, the CEO resigned.
memoryTrick: AFTERMATH literally meant a second mowing/harvest "after" the first — now it means what comes after any major event.
commonMistake: Confusing "aftermath" (the period/effects following an event) with "afterthought" (something considered later, added as an addition) — different concepts entirely.

definition: The gradual wearing down or weakening of something over time, often through sustained pressure or loss.
rootOrigin: Latin "attritio", from "atterere" (to rub against, wear down)
examples: The company faced high attrition as employees left for better offers.
It was a war of attrition, with neither side willing to retreat.
memoryTrick: ATTRITION relates to "rubbing away" — like sandpaper slowly wearing something down.
commonMistake: Confusing "attrition" (gradual wearing down) with "attribution" (crediting a source) — similar start, unrelated meaning.

definition: The quality of being closely connected or appropriate to the matter at hand.
examples: The professor questioned the relevance of the example to the main topic.
Social media has changed the relevance of traditional advertising.
memoryTrick: RELEVANCE relates to something "lifted up" as important and connected to the discussion.
commonMistake: Confusing "relevance" (connection/appropriateness) with "reverence" (deep respect) — similar sound, unrelated meaning.

definition: A feeling or tendency that makes someone want to behave or think in a particular way.
examples: She had a natural inclination toward music from a young age.
He felt no inclination to attend the party.
memoryTrick: INCLINATION relates to "incline" — leaning toward a certain preference or behavior.
commonMistake: Confusing "inclination" (a tendency/preference) with "declination" (a formal refusal, or angular measurement) — related root, different meaning.

definition: Satisfied with one's situation; feeling happy and at peace, not desiring more.
examples: She seemed genuinely content living a quiet life in the countryside.
He was content just to watch from the sidelines.
memoryTrick: CONTENT (adjective) sounds different from CONTENT (noun, meaning the material inside something) — same spelling, different stress and meaning.
commonMistake: Confusing the adjective "conTENT" (satisfied, stress on second syllable) with the noun "CONtent" (subject matter, stress on first syllable) — same spelling, different pronunciation.

definition: Fully in agreement; held or shared by everyone involved without exception.
examples: The board reached a unanimous decision to approve the merger.
The jury was unanimous in finding the defendant not guilty.
memoryTrick: UNANIMOUS = UNUS (one) + ANIMUS (mind) — everyone sharing one single mind or opinion.
commonMistake: Confusing "unanimous" (everyone agreeing) with "anonymous" (unnamed/unknown identity) — similar sound, unrelated meaning.

definition: To frighten or threaten someone, often to make them do something or to make them feel less confident.
examples: The manager tried to intimidate the new employees into working overtime.
She refused to be intimidated by his aggressive tone.
memoryTrick: INTIMIDATE relates to "timid" — making someone feel timid or afraid.
commonMistake: Confusing "intimidate" (to frighten) with "imitate" (to copy) — similar sound, unrelated meaning.

definition: Very great, serious, or intense, especially in a way that causes difficulty, harm, or hardship.
examples: The region experienced severe flooding after days of heavy rain.
He suffered severe injuries in the car accident.
memoryTrick: SEVERE sounds stern and serious — like a strict judge handing down a harsh sentence.
commonMistake: Confusing "severe" (intense/harsh) with "sever" (to cut off completely) — related sound, different meaning and word class.

definition: Wrongful or criminal deception intended to result in financial or personal gain; a person who deceives others.
examples: He was arrested for committing insurance fraud.
The so-called doctor turned out to be a complete fraud.
memoryTrick: FRAUD sounds like "fraught" with deceit — a scheme designed to trick people.
commonMistake: Confusing "fraud" (the act of deception, or a deceiver) with "fraught" (filled with something, often tension) — similar sound, unrelated meaning.

definition: To stimulate or incite a reaction, especially anger or another strong emotion, deliberately.
examples: His comments were clearly meant to provoke a reaction from the crowd.
The court ruled that he had been provoked into responding aggressively.
memoryTrick: PROVOKE = PRO (forth) + VOKE (call) — calling forth a strong reaction from someone.
commonMistake: Confusing "provoke" (to incite a reaction) with "invoke" (to call upon, e.g. a law or spirit) — similar root, different usage.

definition: To bring a feeling, memory, or image to mind.
examples: This old photograph evokes memories of my childhood summers.
The music evoked a deep sense of nostalgia in the audience.
memoryTrick: EVOKE = E (out) + VOKE (call) — calling a memory or feeling out into the open.
commonMistake: Confusing "evoke" (to bring to mind) with "invoke" (to call upon formally, e.g. a rule or higher power) — similar root, different direction of use.

definition: To wake someone up, or to stir a person into action or a strong emotional state.
rootOrigin: Middle English, possibly falconry term for a bird shaking its feathers
examples: The alarm finally roused him from a deep sleep.
Her rousing speech motivated the entire team before the final match.
memoryTrick: ROUSE sounds like "arouse" — stirring someone from sleep or inaction into alertness.
commonMistake: Confusing "rouse" (to wake/stir up) with "rose" (past tense of rise, or the flower) — similar sound, unrelated meaning.

definition: To escape or avoid something, especially by cleverness, cunning, or deception.
examples: The suspect managed to evade police for several days.
She skillfully evaded the reporter's difficult questions.
memoryTrick: EVADE = E (out) + VADE (go) — going out of the way to escape something.
commonMistake: Confusing "evade" (to avoid cleverly) with "invade" (to enter forcefully) — opposite direction of movement despite the shared root "-vade".

definition: To formally and officially end, repeal, or cancel a law, agreement, or right.
examples: The treaty was abrogated in 1949 after years of dispute.
The new government moved quickly to abrogate the previous administration's policies.
memoryTrick: ABROGATE = AB (away) + ROGATE (propose a law) — taking a law away after it was proposed.
commonMistake: Confusing "abrogate" (to formally cancel a law/agreement) with "arrogate" (to claim something without right) — similar spelling, very different meaning.

definition: To refuse to accept or be associated with something; to deny the validity of a claim, agreement, or belief.
examples: He repudiated the allegations made against him in the report.
The country repudiated its debt after the economic collapse.
memoryTrick: REPUDIATE relates to a formal, almost legal, act of rejection — like refusing to honor a contract.
commonMistake: Confusing "repudiate" (to formally reject/deny) with "repute" (reputation) — similar beginning, unrelated modern meaning.

definition: To sit, lean, or fall heavily and limply, especially with a bent back.
rootOrigin: Unknown origin, possibly related to 'slip' or 'plump'
examples: He was so tired he just slumped onto the sofa.
memoryTrick: SLUMP sounds like a heavy PLUMP drop.
commonMistake: Confusing it with an economic 'slump' (which is related but means a decline).

definition: To estimate or determine the magnitude, amount, or volume of something; an instrument for measuring.
examples: He looked at the gauges on the dashboard to check the fuel.
memoryTrick: A GAUGE gives you the AGE or amount of something accurately.
commonMistake: Misspelling as 'guage' (remember A before U: G-A-U-G-E).

definition: Quoting (a passage, book, or author) as evidence for or justification of an argument or statement.
examples: She was citing a famous poem in her speech.
memoryTrick: CITE sounds like SIGHT; you CITE things you have SIGHTed in books.
commonMistake: Confusing 'cite' with 'site' (location) or 'sight' (vision).

definition: Tending to move unsteadily from side to side.
examples: The newborn calf stood on wobbly legs.
memoryTrick: Wobbly things 'wobble' (shake) like jelly.
commonMistake: Using it for abstract emotional instability (it is mostly physical).

definition: To move (one's head) from side to side in order to indicate refusal, denial, or disapproval.
antonyms: Nod (in the context of head movement)
examples: He shook his head when I asked if he was okay.
memoryTrick: Shake is like a snake moving side to side.
commonMistake: Confusing the past tense forms (shook, shaken).

definition: To hold on tightly to.
examples: She clung tightly to her mother's hand.
memoryTrick: CLING sounds like a CLAW digging IN to hold on.
commonMistake: Using regular past tense 'clinged' instead of 'clung'.

definition: Staying close; tending to depend too much on another person for emotional support.
examples: He broke up with her because she was too clingy.
memoryTrick: Someone who is clingy is always trying to CLING onto you.
commonMistake: Using it as a compliment (it usually has a negative/annoying connotation).

definition: Of, from, or like God or a god; excellent, delightful.
context: Mentioned but definition unwritten in notes. General meaning applied.
examples: The choir's performance was absolutely divine.
memoryTrick: DIVINE -> related to Deities (Gods).
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'devine'.

definition: So delicate or precise as to be difficult to analyze or describe; understated.
examples: There was a subtle change in his attitude.
memoryTrick: The 'b' in subtle is subtle (silent).
commonMistake: Pronouncing the 'b'. It should be pronounced 'sut-ul'.

definition: Having mixed feelings or contradictory ideas about something or someone (कभी इधर कभी उधर).
context: कभी इधर कभी उधर (Sometimes here, sometimes there).
examples: He was ambivalent about moving to a new city.
memoryTrick: AMBI (both) + VALENT (value). You see value in BOTH choices, so you can't decide.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'ambiguous' (unclear meaning) or 'indifferent' (not caring at all).

definition: Dealing with things sensibly and realistically in a way that is based on practical rather than theoretical considerations.
examples: We need a pragmatic approach to solving the budget crisis.
memoryTrick: PRAGMATIC sounds like PRACTICAL. Both mean focusing on real-world actions.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'dogmatic' (rigidly following rules).

definition: An imagined place or state of things in which everything is perfect; a dream ideal world.
rootOrigin: Greek 'ou' (not) + 'topos' (place) - coined by Thomas More, meaning 'no place'.
examples: The novel describes a futuristic utopia where poverty has been eliminated.
memoryTrick: U TOPIA -> YOU TOP. A place where you are at the top/perfect condition.
commonMistake: Using it as an adjective (the adjective is utopian).

definition: An imagined state or society in which there is great suffering or injustice, typically one that is totalitarian or post-apocalyptic.
examples: The movie is set in a bleak dystopia ruled by corporations.
memoryTrick: DYS (dysfunctional/bad) + TOPIA (place). A bad place.
commonMistake: Spelling it with an 'i' instead of 'y' (distopia).

definition: Putting (someone or something) into a situation in which there is a danger of loss, harm, or failure; endangering.
context: Notes: jeopardy (n), Put someone into a situation of danger, Endangering, imperiling.
rootOrigin: French 'jeu parti' (divided game, meaning uncertain chance)
examples: He would never do anything to jeopardise his career.
memoryTrick: Jeopardise sounds like LEOPARD-ize. Putting someone near a leopard puts them in danger.
commonMistake: Spelling with 'ize' vs 'ise' (both are correct depending on US/UK, but 'jeopardise' is British spelling).

definition: To declare one's public approval or support of.
rootOrigin: Latin 'in' (on) + 'dorsum' (back) - literally writing on the back of a check.
examples: The famous athlete was paid to endorse the new sneakers.
memoryTrick: Endorse has 'door' in it; opening the door to support someone.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'indorse' (which is a valid but very rare spelling).

definition: Swelling or expanding out in a spherical shape; increasing rapidly.
examples: The company is struggling to manage its ballooning debt.
memoryTrick: Like a balloon filling with air, expanding rapidly.
commonMistake: Misspelling with one 'l' or one 'o'.

definition: A state in which two things are able to exist or occur together without problems or conflict.
context: Written as 'compatability' in notes. Comparable, harmony, accord, congruence.
examples: They divorced due to a complete lack of compatibility.
memoryTrick: If you are COMPATIBLE, you can COMBAT (deal with) life's problems together.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'compatability' (should be an 'i' after the 't').

definition: A typical example or pattern of something; a model.
examples: The discovery of DNA created a new paradigm in biology.
memoryTrick: Paradigm has a silent G, it's a PARA (perfect) DIGM (dime) -> a perfect example.
commonMistake: Pronouncing the 'g' or spelling it without the 'g'.

definition: Savagely violent; cruel.
examples: The dictator was known for his brutal regime.
memoryTrick: BRUTAL sounds like BRUTE (a savage beast).
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'fatal' (causing death).

definition: In poor health; suffering, weak.
examples: He stayed home to care for his ailing wife.
memoryTrick: AILING comes from AIL (ill). Someone who is ailing is ill.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'alien'.

definition: Walking or moving at a slow, relaxed pace.
examples: We spent the afternoon ambling through the park.
memoryTrick: AMBULANCE comes from the same root (to move/walk), but an amble is a slow walk, unlike an ambulance.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'rambling' (which can mean talking aimlessly).

definition: A person or animal with whom one spends a lot of time or with whom one travels.
context: A person (Spend a lot of time)
rootOrigin: Latin 'com' (together with) + 'panis' (bread) -> someone you break bread with.
examples: His dog became his closest companion after he moved.
memoryTrick: COM (together) + PAN (bread) = someone you eat with, a good friend.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'companian'.

definition: To become limp through heat, loss of water, or disease; droop or sag.
rootOrigin: Late 17th century dialectal variation of 'welk' (lose freshness).
examples: The flowers wilted in the hot sun.
memoryTrick: When things WILT, they 'will' eventually die if not watered.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'willed' (used willpower).

definition: To look quickly and furtively at something, especially through a narrow opening.
context: to have a look at, see watch
examples: She peeped through the keyhole to see who was inside.
memoryTrick: PEEP looks like two eyes (ee) looking through a slit (P P).
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'peeked' (though they are nearly synonymous).

definition: To throw (an object) with great force.
examples: Rioters hurled stones at the police.
memoryTrick: HURL rhymes with WHIRL. You whirl your arm back to hurl something.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'hurtled' (to move at great speed).

definition: Calm, peaceful, and untroubled; tranquil.
examples: The lake was completely serene early in the morning.
memoryTrick: SERENE sounds like a SCENE from nature that is quiet and beautiful.
commonMistake: Using it to describe exciting situations.

definition: Heavily loaded or weighed down; filled.
examples: The trees were laden with apples.
memoryTrick: LADEN sounds like LOADED in.
commonMistake: Confusing it with the name 'Bin Laden'. Use it as a descriptor (e.g., guilt-laden).

definition: (Of a disease or physical abnormality) present from birth; inherited.
examples: The baby was born with a congenital heart condition.
memoryTrick: CON (with) + GENITAL (genes/birth). You have it from the genes/birth.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'congenial' (pleasant).

definition: (Of a person) pleasant because of a personality, qualities, or interests that are similar to one's own; like-minded.
examples: We spent a relaxed evening with congenial friends.
memoryTrick: CONGENIAL -> 'GENIAL' means friendly. A genial person is congenial.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'congenital' (present from birth).

definition: Strong hostility or enmity.
examples: There is a long history of animosity between the two nations.
memoryTrick: ANIMOSITY -> ANIMAL-SITY. Acting like wild animals toward each other in a city.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'animosaty'.

definition: Deliberately cruel or violent; harmful.
examples: He suffered a vicious attack from a stray dog.
memoryTrick: VICE = bad habit. VICIOUS = full of bad/evil.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'viscous' (thick liquid).

definition: Physical or mental weakness; fragility.
examples: Despite her advancing years and infirmity, she remained cheerful.
memoryTrick: IN (not) + FIRM (strong). Not strong = weak.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'infirmary' (a hospital).

definition: Having a solid, almost unyielding surface or structure; stable, determined.
examples: He was firm in his belief that he had made the right choice.
memoryTrick: If a mattress is FIRM, it is stable and solid. A firm decision is solid.
commonMistake: Confusing the adjective (solid) with the noun (a company).

definition: Rough and bad-mannered; uncivilized, uncouth.
rootOrigin: Dutch 'boer' (farmer/peasant) -> viewed by city folk as unrefined.
examples: His boorish behavior at the dinner party embarrassed his wife.
memoryTrick: BOO! People boo at boorish (rude) behavior.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'boring'.

definition: The ability to do something successfully or efficiently; capacity.
examples: The job requires a high level of technical competence.
memoryTrick: If you COMPETE well, you have COMPETENCE.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'competance'.

definition: (Of an amount) small or meager; insignificant.
context: Insignificant, small (miswritten as 'Party' in initial scan)
examples: He was paid a paltry sum for his hard work.
memoryTrick: POULTRY (chickens) are small birds. PALTRY means small/insignificant.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'poultry'.

definition: Extremely unpleasant; nasty, wicked.
examples: The villain committed a vile act of betrayal.
memoryTrick: VILE is an anagram of EVIL.
commonMistake: Using it for mild dislikes (it is a very strong word).

definition: A profoundly wise person; wise, especially as a result of great experience.
examples: The villagers went to the sage for advice on the harvest.
memoryTrick: SAGEs say wise things. (Also a herb, but memory trick is wisdom).
commonMistake: Confusing it strictly with the herb in non-culinary contexts.

definition: Not severe, serious, or harsh; gentle.
examples: He suffered a mild heart attack but is recovering well.
memoryTrick: MILD weather makes you SMILE.
commonMistake: None typically.

definition: Not honorable in character or purpose; shameful, vile, causing meanness.
examples: Stealing from the charity was a truly ignoble deed.
memoryTrick: IG (not) + NOBLE. Someone who is not noble is ignoble.
commonMistake: Misunderstanding it as 'ignorant'.

definition: Of little value or importance; insignificant.
examples: We shouldn't argue over such trivial matters.
memoryTrick: TRIVIA games are about small, unimportant facts (trivial).
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'triveal'.

definition: The feeling that a person or a thing is beneath consideration, worthless, or deserving scorn; hate, loathe.
examples: He spoke with contempt for the rules of the club.
memoryTrick: CONTEMPT -> When you condemn someone, you treat them with contempt.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'attempt'.

definition: Used to emphasize that a quality or fact, typically a bad one, is well known.
examples: The company is notoriously slow at paying its bills.
memoryTrick: Notorious = Noted for something serious/bad.
commonMistake: Using it for someone famous for good reasons.

definition: So as to give the impression of having a certain quality; apparently.
examples: It was a seemingly impossible task, but they completed it.
memoryTrick: It SEEMS like it is true = Seemingly.
commonMistake: Using it when confirming an absolute fact.

definition: Carefully chosen and thoughtfully organized or presented.
examples: The museum features a beautifully curated selection of modern art.
memoryTrick: A CURATOR takes CARE of organizing the museum.
commonMistake: Overusing it to describe basic lists.

definition: Inborn; natural.
examples: She has an innate talent for music.
memoryTrick: IN (in) + NATE (native/born) = born with it inside you.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'inane' (silly).

definition: Keep from sight; hide.
examples: He tried to conceal his identity with a heavy coat and hat.
memoryTrick: ConCEAL - you put a SEAL on it to hide it.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'conceil'.

definition: Self-evident or unquestionable; obvious.
examples: It is axiomatic that a healthy diet is good for you.
memoryTrick: AXIOM is a known fact in math. Axiomatic means obviously true.
commonMistake: Using it for opinions rather than universally accepted facts.

definition: To live in or at a specified place; to think, speak, or write at length about a particular subject, especially one that is a source of unhappiness.
rootOrigin: Old English 'dwellan' (to lead astray, hinder, delay).
examples: They dwell in the forest.
Try not to dwell on your mistakes.
memoryTrick: DWELL sounds like WELL; people used to live (dwell) near a well.
commonMistake: Using 'dwell' without 'on' or 'upon' when talking about thinking about something.

definition: To remove something, or to subtract a number; a key fact or idea to be remembered.
rootOrigin: Combination of Old English 'tacan' (to grasp) and 'aweg' (away).
examples: Take away the empty plates, please.
The main takeaway from the meeting was positive.
memoryTrick: Literally taking something and putting it away from its current location.
commonMistake: Confusing the phrasal verb 'take away' with the noun 'takeaway'.

definition: The state of having knowledge or understanding; the action of enlightening or the state of being enlightened.
examples: He traveled to India in search of spiritual enlightenment.
The book provided some enlightenment on the complex issue.
memoryTrick: EN-LIGHT-EN-MENT: Bringing 'light' (knowledge) into your mind.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'enlightment' instead of 'enlightenment'.

definition: As an alternative or substitute.
examples: I will have tea instead of coffee.
She didn't go to the party; instead, she stayed home.
memoryTrick: IN-STEAD: In the 'stead' (place) of something else.
commonMistake: Using 'instead of' at the end of a sentence instead of just 'instead'.

definition: Used to express uncertainty or possibility.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from 'per' (by) + plural of 'hap' (chance).
examples: Perhaps it will rain tomorrow.
He is perhaps the best player on the team.
memoryTrick: PER-HAPS: Per (by) haps (happening/chance).
commonMistake: Using 'perhaps' when you are completely sure of something.

definition: The quality of having experience, knowledge, and good judgment; the quality of being wise.
examples: Listen to the wisdom of your elders.
She had the wisdom to walk away from the argument.
memoryTrick: WIS-DOM: The DOMain of the WISe.
commonMistake: Confusing wisdom (knowing how to use knowledge) with mere intelligence (having knowledge).

definition: Difficulties; misfortune.
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French 'aversite', from Latin 'adversitas', from 'adversus' (against).
examples: She showed great resilience in the face of adversity.
Adversity often brings out the best in people.
memoryTrick: AD-VERS-ITY: Things going 'in reverse' or 'against' (adverse) your plans.
commonMistake: Confusing 'adversity' (hardship) with 'adversary' (opponent).

definition: Separated by a distance; of two or more people or things.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Old French 'a part' (to one side).
examples: They stood feet apart.
Apart from the weather, the holiday was great.
memoryTrick: A-PART: Literally meaning 'a part' or separate from the whole.
commonMistake: Confusing 'apart' (separated) with 'a part' (a piece of).

definition: Improve or enhance the quality or value of.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Old French 'enrichir', from 'en-' (in) + 'riche' (rich).
examples: The soil is enriched with natural fertilizers.
Her life was enriched by her travels.
memoryTrick: EN-RICH: To make something 'rich' or full of value.
commonMistake: Using 'enriched' when you simply mean 'increased' in quantity, rather than quality.

definition: A line or border separating two countries; the extreme limit of understanding or achievement in a particular area.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Old French 'frontiere', from 'front' (front).
examples: They are exploring the frontiers of space.
The river acts as a frontier between the two nations.
memoryTrick: FRONT-IERS: The 'front' line of a territory or field of study.
commonMistake: Misspelling as 'frontiers' instead of 'frontiers' (wait, that's correct), spelling as 'frontires'.

definition: So as to give the impression of having a certain quality; apparently.
rootOrigin: From 'seem' + '-ing' + '-ly'. Old Norse 'sœma' (to honor, conform to).
examples: It was a seemingly impossible task.
He remained seemingly calm during the crisis.
memoryTrick: SEEM-ING-LY: How something 'seems' to be on the outside.
commonMistake: Confusing seemingly (appearance) with definitely (reality).

definition: The quality or state of being mediocre; ordinary or of moderate quality.
rootOrigin: Late 16th century: from French 'médiocrité' or Latin 'mediocritas', from 'medius' (middle) + 'ocris' (rugged mountain).
examples: He refused to accept mediocrity in his team.
The film was a triumph of style over mediocrity.
memoryTrick: MEDIO sounds like 'medium'. Mediocrity is being stuck in the middle, not great.
commonMistake: Misspelling as 'mediocrety'.

definition: The intrinsic nature or indispensable quality of something, especially something abstract, that determines its character.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Latin 'essentia', from 'esse' (to be).
examples: Time is of the essence.
The essence of the argument is that education should be free.
memoryTrick: ESS-ENCE: The 'essential' piece.
commonMistake: Using 'essence' when referring to a physical extract instead of abstract core (though it can mean extract, 'essential oil' is more common).

definition: In a profoundly wise manner.
rootOrigin: From 'sage' + '-ly'. 'Sage' from Old French, from Latin 'sapere' (to be wise).
examples: He nodded sagely as she explained the problem.
She spoke sagely about the importance of patience.
memoryTrick: SAGE-LY: Acting like a 'sage' (a wise old person).
commonMistake: Confusing it with the herb 'sage' contextually.

definition: Strengthen or support (an object or substance), especially with additional material.
examples: The building was reinforced with steel.
He used examples to reinforce his argument.
memoryTrick: RE-IN-FORCE: To put force 'in' again (re).
commonMistake: Spelling it as 're-enforce' (reinforce is standard).

definition: Lower and raise one's head slightly and briefly, especially in greeting, assent, or understanding, or to give someone a signal.
context: झुकना / down (अपनी ओर नीचे की ओर)
examples: She nodded in agreement.
Anna nodded over her book, nearly asleep.
memoryTrick: NOD-DED: Head goes up and down.
commonMistake: Confusing 'nodding' (yes) with 'shaking head' (no).

definition: Sleepy and lethargic; half asleep.
context: Sleepy / सुस्ती (Head falls forward asleep) (सिर हिलाना)
rootOrigin: Mid 16th century: from obsolete 'drowse' (be sleepy), of unknown origin.
examples: The medication might make you feel drowsy.
It was a warm, drowsy afternoon.
memoryTrick: DROWSY sounds like 'down-sy' - your eyes want to go down.
commonMistake: Using 'drowsy' to mean physically exhausted rather than just sleepy.

definition: Sleeping lightly.
rootOrigin: Mid 17th century: probably of Scandinavian origin; related to Danish 'døse' (make drowsy).
examples: He was dozing in front of the television.
I kept dozing off during the lecture.
memoryTrick: DOZE rhymes with NOSE; you breathe heavily through your nose when you doze.
commonMistake: Confusing 'dozing' (light sleep) with 'sleeping' (deep sleep).

definition: Involuntarily open one's mouth wide and inhale deeply due to tiredness or boredom; (of a hole or space) gaping open.
examples: He couldn't stop yawning during the meeting.
There was a yawning gap between the rich and the poor.
memoryTrick: YAWN: You All Want Naps.
commonMistake: Using 'yawning' only for sleepiness, forgetting it can describe wide open spaces (e.g., yawning chasm).

definition: Regard as probable; expect or predict.
rootOrigin: Mid 16th century: from Latin 'anticipat-' (acted in advance), from 'anti-' (before) + 'capere' (to take).
examples: We don't anticipate any trouble.
She anticipated his question and had the answer ready.
memoryTrick: ANTI-CIPATE: Acting 'anti' (before) something happens.
commonMistake: Confusing 'anticipate' (take action expecting something) with just 'expect' (waiting for it).

definition: Hold royal office; rule as monarch. The period during which a sovereign rules.
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French 'reignier', from Latin 'regnare', from 'rex, reg-' (king).
examples: Queen Elizabeth II had a long reign.
Silence reigned in the empty room.
memoryTrick: REIGN sounds like RAIN; a king's power falls over his people like rain.
commonMistake: Confusing 'reign' (rule) with 'rein' (horse strap) or 'rain' (water).

definition: People inclined to question or doubt accepted opinions.
rootOrigin: Mid 16th century: from French 'sceptique', or via Latin from Greek 'skeptikos' (inquiring, reflecting).
examples: The sceptics argued that the plan would never work.
He was a sceptic about the new technology.
memoryTrick: SCEPTIC sounds like IN-SPECT; they inspect claims because they doubt them.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'skeptic' (US English) vs 'sceptic' (UK English) without consistency.

definition: In a way that is neatly skillful and quick in movement.
examples: She deftly avoided the question.
His fingers moved deftly over the keys.
memoryTrick: DEFT sounds like THEFT; a thief must move deftly to not get caught.
commonMistake: Confusing deftly with 'daftly' (foolishly).

definition: A system of religious veneration and devotion directed toward a particular figure or object.
rootOrigin: Early 17th century: from French culte or Latin cultus 'worship'.
examples: He was rescued from a dangerous cult.
The movie has developed a cult following.
memoryTrick: CULTure taken to an extreme becomes a CULT.
commonMistake: Using 'cult' just to mean any religion, rather than an extreme/fringe one.

definition: Grow or develop in a healthy or vigorous way, especially as the result of a particularly favorable environment.
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French floriss-, lengthened stem of florir, based on Latin flos, flor- 'flower'.
examples: Wild flowers flourish on the bank.
The economy flourished under the new government.
memoryTrick: FLOURISH: Think of a FLOWER-ish plant growing big and healthy.
commonMistake: Confusing flourish (grow well) with flaunt (show off).

definition: Spread throughout (something); pervade.
rootOrigin: Mid 17th century: from Latin permeat- 'passed through'.
examples: The aroma of soup was permeating the air.
Corruption is permeating the system.
memoryTrick: PER-MEAT: Think of marinade going completely through (per) the MEAT.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'permiating'.

definition: Rapid increase in numbers.
rootOrigin: Mid 19th century: from French prolifération, from Latin proles 'offspring'.
examples: We are trying to stop the proliferation of nuclear weapons.
There has been a proliferation of coffee shops in the area.
memoryTrick: PRO-LIFE-RATION: Pro (supporting) life generating rapidly in rations/numbers.
commonMistake: Confusing it with mere 'growth'—proliferation specifically means a *rapid* spread or increase.

definition: A countless or extremely great number.
rootOrigin: Mid 16th century: via late Latin from Greek murias, muriad-, from murios '10,000'.
examples: There are a myriad of options available.
He has myriad ideas for the project.
memoryTrick: MY-RIAD: My radio has a MYRIAD (huge number) of channels.
commonMistake: Saying 'myriads of' instead of 'a myriad of' or just 'myriad'.

definition: The action of reducing the severity, seriousness, or painfulness of something.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Latin mitigatio(n-), from the verb mitigare 'soften, alleviate'.
examples: The mitigation of climate change is a global priority.
His lawyer pleaded for mitigation of the sentence.
memoryTrick: MITI-GATE: Making the GATE less hard to pass through (reducing severity).
commonMistake: Confusing 'mitigate' (reduce severity) with 'militate' (be a powerful factor against).

definition: Expressing the opinion that something is of little worth; derogatory.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Old French desparagier 'marry someone of unequal rank', from des- (expressing reversal) + parage 'lineage'.
examples: He made disparaging remarks about his competitors.
She was tired of his disparaging attitude.
memoryTrick: DIS-PAR: Saying someone is not on PAR (standard), thus putting them down.
commonMistake: Confusing disparaging with discouraging.

definition: Make (someone) stronger and more confident, especially in controlling their life and claiming their rights.
examples: It was an empowering experience.
The program aims at empowering youth.
memoryTrick: EM-POWER: Putting POWER into someone.
commonMistake: Overusing the buzzword 'empowering' without explaining how power is gained.

definition: Having a calming or sedative effect.
examples: The music had a tranquillising effect on the baby.
They used a tranquillising dart on the lion.
memoryTrick: TRANQUIL = Calm. Tranquillising = Making calm.
commonMistake: Spelling it with one 'l' (tranquilizing) is US English, two 'l's (tranquillising) is UK English.

definition: Following a twisting or spiral course.
rootOrigin: Old English windan 'go in a circular course'.
examples: We drove down a long and winding road.
The river is winding through the valley.
memoryTrick: WINDING like the WIND that changes direction constantly.
commonMistake: Pronouncing it like the wind (air) /wɪndɪŋ/ instead of /waɪndɪŋ/.

definition: A brief or temporary failure of concentration, memory, or judgment.
rootOrigin: Late 15th century: from Latin lapsus 'slipping, falling'.
examples: He had some serious memory lapses.
There were several lapses in security.
memoryTrick: LAPSE: A gap like laps running around a track—sometimes you miss a lap (gap).
commonMistake: Using 'lapse' when meaning a permanent failure; a lapse is usually temporary.

definition: Describe or portray (something) precisely.
rootOrigin: Mid 16th century: from Latin delineat- 'outlined', from de- 'out, completely' + linea 'line'.
examples: The law should delineate and prohibit behavior which is socially abhorrent.
Her roles are clearly delineated.
memoryTrick: DE-LINE-ATE: To trace the LINES of something to describe it.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'alienate'.

definition: Spreading widely throughout an area or a group of people.
rootOrigin: Mid 18th century: from Latin pervas- 'passed through'.
examples: Ageism is pervasive and entrenched in our society.
There is a pervasive smell of dampness.
memoryTrick: PER-VASIVE: Sounds like INVASIVE, something that spreads everywhere.
commonMistake: Confusing 'pervasive' (widespread) with 'persuasive' (convincing).

definition: Not securely held or in position; dangerously likely to fall or collapse. Dependent on chance; uncertain.
rootOrigin: Mid 17th century: from Latin precarius 'obtained by entreaty', from prex, prec- 'prayer'.
examples: He made a precarious living as a painter.
The ladder looked very precarious.
memoryTrick: PRE-CARE-IOUS: You need to take PRE-CARE before handling something precarious.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'precarius'.

definition: In its original condition; unspoiled.
examples: The car is in pristine condition.
They want to preserve the pristine nature of the forest.
memoryTrick: PRISTINE sounds like PRIEST, who is considered pure and uncorrupted.
commonMistake: Using pristine just to mean 'clean' rather than 'in its original, flawless state'.

definition: Easily broken or damaged.
rootOrigin: Early 16th century: from French, or from Latin fragilis, from frangere 'to break'.
examples: Be careful, those glasses are very fragile.
The economy remains in a fragile state.
memoryTrick: FRAGILE items easily FRAGMENT into pieces.
commonMistake: Pronouncing it like 'fra-jeel' instead of 'fra-jyle'.

definition: Intrusion on a person's territory, rights, etc.
rootOrigin: Early 16th century: from Old French encrochier 'seize, fasten on'.
examples: They protested against the encroachment of their privacy.
The urban encroachment destroyed the wildlife habitat.
memoryTrick: EN-CROACH: Think of a ROACH invading your kitchen.
commonMistake: Forgetting the 'n' in the spelling: ecroachment.

definition: Having the ability to restore health, strength, or a feeling of well-being.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Old French restoratif, -ive, from the verb restorer.
examples: A restorative walk in the fresh air.
She felt the restorative power of sleep.
memoryTrick: RESTORE-ATIVE: Has the power to restore.
commonMistake: Confusing restorative (healing) with restrictive (limiting).

definition: A generally accepted theory or belief.
context: a generally theory accepted or belief
rootOrigin: Term popularized by John Kenneth Galbraith in 1958.
examples: Conventional wisdom says that you shouldn't eat before swimming.
The startup defied conventional wisdom and succeeded.
memoryTrick: CONVENTIONAL (standard) WISDOM (what people know).
commonMistake: Assuming conventional wisdom is always factually true; it is just what is widely believed.

definition: An inclination or natural tendency to behave in a particular way.
rootOrigin: Late 16th century: from Latin propensus 'inclined', from pro- 'forward' + pendere 'hang'.
examples: He has a propensity for violence.
Their propensity to consume more than they earn is alarming.
memoryTrick: PROPENSITY: Sounds like PRO-PENCIL; a writer has a natural tendency or propensity to hold a pencil.
commonMistake: Confusing propensity with capacity; propensity is a tendency, capacity is an ability.

definition: A large or dense group of flying insects.
context: a large or dense group of flying insects
rootOrigin: Old English swearm, of Germanic origin.
examples: A swarm of bees flew into the garden.
The reporters were a swarm around the celebrity.
memoryTrick: SWARM sounds like WARM; insects gather in a swarm when it is warm.
commonMistake: Using 'swarm' for inanimate objects when it's primarily used for living things in motion.

definition: Move in or form a swarm.
context: move in or form a swarm
rootOrigin: Old English swearm, of Germanic origin.
examples: The children swarmed into the playground.
The beach is swarming with tourists.
memoryTrick: SWARM: When many people gather together like a flock of insects.
commonMistake: Forgetting that 'swarm with' means to be crowded or overrun with.

definition: Issue or spread out from (a source).
rootOrigin: Mid 18th century: from Latin emanat- 'flowed out', from e- 'out' + manare 'to flow'.
examples: A beautiful scent emanates from the flowers.
Warmth emanates from the fireplace.
memoryTrick: EMANATE: Think of 'E-MAN' (a superhero) who radiates or issues energy out.
commonMistake: Confusing 'emanate' with 'imitate'.

definition: Separate or release (someone or something) from something to which they are attached or connected.
rootOrigin: Early 17th century: from French désengager, from dés- (expressing reversal) + engager 'pledge'.
examples: He tried to disengage himself from the awkward conversation.
You need to disengage the gears.
memoryTrick: DIS-ENGAGE: The opposite of engaging or connecting.
commonMistake: Using disengage when meaning to ignore; disengage requires a prior connection to separate from.

definition: Leave (a place that one previously occupied).
rootOrigin: Mid 17th century: from Latin vacat- 'left empty', from the verb vacare.
examples: Guests must vacate their rooms by 11 AM.
They were forced to vacate the building.
memoryTrick: VACATE: Make it VACANT.
commonMistake: Confusing 'vacate' with 'vacation'; though related by root, one means leaving a specific place, the other means taking a holiday.

definition: Showing or having an insensitive and cruel disregard for others.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Latin callosus 'hard-skinned', from callus.
examples: His callous comments hurt her feelings deeply.
The thieves showed a callous disregard for human life.
memoryTrick: CALLOUS: A callus is hard skin; a callous person has a hard, unfeeling heart.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'callus' which is the noun form for hard skin, rather than the adjective for behavior.

definition: Tease or laugh at in a scornful or contemptuous manner.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Old French mocquer 'deride'.
examples: He was mocked for his strange clothes.
The opposition mocked the government's attempts.
memoryTrick: MOCK: When you make fun of someone, making them look like a fake or a 'mock-up'.
commonMistake: Using 'mock' to mean completely fake without negative context (e.g., mock exam), though true, 'mocked' as a verb implies teasing.

definition: Rise in opposition or armed resistance to an established government or ruler.
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French rebeller, from Latin rebellare, from re- 'again' + bellare 'wage war'.
examples: The citizens rebelled against the unfair taxes.
As a teenager, she rebelled against her parents' strict rules.
memoryTrick: RE-BELL: Ringing the BELL again to start a fight or resistance.
commonMistake: Pronunciation shifts: REB-el (noun) vs re-BELL (verb).

definition: Refuse to obey the orders of a person in authority.
rootOrigin: Mid 16th century: from obsolete French mutiner, from mutin 'rebellious'.
examples: The crew mutinied and took control of the ship.
The soldiers mutinied against their commanding officer.
memoryTrick: MUTINY: Think of the MUTANTS in X-Men who rebelled against authority.
commonMistake: Using mutiny in everyday situations; it is usually reserved for military or ship-based rebellion.

definition: Openly resist or refuse to obey.
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French defier, from de- (expressing reversal) + Latin fidus 'faithful'.
examples: She defied her parents and dropped out of school.
His recovery defied all medical logic.
memoryTrick: DEFY: DE-Faith. Breaking faith or refusing to bow down.
commonMistake: Confusing 'defy' with 'deify' (to treat as a god).

definition: Make a gap in and break through (a wall, barrier, or defense).
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French breche, from the Germanic base of break.
examples: The river breached its banks.
They successfully sued the company that breached the contract.
memoryTrick: BREACH sounds like BREAK; to breach a wall is to break through it.
commonMistake: Confusing 'breach' (break through) with 'breech' (the back part of a rifle barrel or a type of birth).

definition: Pleasure, especially when gained from the satisfaction of a desire.
rootOrigin: Mid 16th century: from Latin gratificatio(n-), from the verb gratificari 'give or do as a favor'.
examples: We live in a culture of instant gratification.
He smiled with deep gratification.
memoryTrick: GRATIFICATION comes from being GRATEFUL for satisfaction.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'gratifaction'.

definition: The action of following or pursuing someone or something.
context: the action of trying to achieve
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French purseute, from pursewre 'to pursue'.
examples: They drove off in pursuit of the stolen car.
She traveled the world in pursuit of her dreams.
memoryTrick: PURSUIT: PURSUE IT. Go after it to achieve it.
commonMistake: Using 'pursuit' as a verb. (Pursue is the verb).

definition: Existing or coming before in time, order, or importance.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Latin prior 'former, earlier'.
examples: I have no prior knowledge of this event.
Please arrive 15 minutes prior to your appointment.
memoryTrick: PRIOR is just BEFORE.
commonMistake: Saying 'prior than' instead of 'prior to'.

definition: Give or ascribe value or validity to (something).
rootOrigin: Early 20th century: from French valoriser, from valeur 'value'.
examples: The culture tends to valorise wealth above all else.
We should valorise the contributions of all workers.
memoryTrick: VALORISE contains VALOR/VALUE, meaning to give something value.
commonMistake: US spelling is 'valorize', UK is 'valorise'. Do not confuse with 'valor' (bravery).

definition: Deliver a sermon or religious address to an assembled group of people; earnestly advocate (a belief or course of action).
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French preechier, from Latin praedicare 'proclaim'.
examples: The minister will preach on forgiveness.
My parents are always preaching to me about saving money.
memoryTrick: PREACH: P-REACH out to people with your words.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'teach'; preach has a stronger moral or religious connotation.

definition: The crime of betraying one's country, especially by attempting to kill the sovereign or overthrow the government.
context: showing no loyalty / act harm to your country
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Anglo-Norman French tresoun, from Latin traditio(n-) 'handing over'.
examples: He was executed for treason.
Selling state secrets is an act of high treason.
memoryTrick: TREASON sounds like REASON. Betraying your country defies all REASON.
commonMistake: Confusing 'treason' (against one's country) with 'treachery' (general betrayal).

definition: Go somewhere with (someone) as a companion or escort.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Old French accompagner, from a- (to) + compaignon 'companion'.
examples: He was accompanied by his wife.
The thunderstorm was accompanied by high winds.
memoryTrick: ACCOMPANIED: Having COMPANY.
commonMistake: Spelling it with one 'c' or one 'm' (acompained).

definition: Implied though not plainly expressed.
context: understood but not put into clearly words.
rootOrigin: Late 16th century: from French implicite or Latin implicitus 'entangled', from implicare.
examples: There was an implicit agreement between them.
She had implicit trust in his abilities.
memoryTrick: IMPLICIT: It's IN-plied (Implied), not put OUT there (Explicit).
commonMistake: Confusing implicit (implied) with explicit (clearly stated).

definition: Of, from, or like God or a god.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Old French devin, from Latin divinus, from divus 'godlike'.
examples: They believed in the divine right of kings.
The cake tasted absolutely divine.
memoryTrick: DIVINE: Connecting with the DIVINITY or the DEITY.
commonMistake: Using 'divine' only to mean 'very good' and forgetting its primary religious meaning.

definition: A principle or belief, especially one of the main principles of a religion or philosophy.
rootOrigin: Late 16th century: from Latin tenet 'he holds', from the verb tenere.
examples: Non-violence is one of the core tenets of their faith.
The tenets of democracy are important.
memoryTrick: TENETS: Ten ants (principles) holding the foundation together.
commonMistake: Confusing 'tenet' (belief) with 'tenant' (person who rents).

definition: Form or devise (a plan or idea) in the mind.
context: to think New idea or plan.
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French concevoir, from Latin concipere, from con- 'together' + capere 'take'.
examples: He conceived a brilliant plan to save the company.
I can hardly conceive of such a thing.
memoryTrick: CONCEIVE: To receive a concept into your mind.
commonMistake: Forgetting the 'i before e except after c' rule (spelling it concieve).

definition: Be an expression of or give a tangible or visible form to (an idea, quality, or feeling).
examples: He embodies the spirit of the team.
The new constitution embodies democratic principles.
memoryTrick: EM-BODY: To put an abstract idea into a physical BODY (or form).
commonMistake: Confusing embody with embolden (give courage).

definition: A kingdom; a field or domain of activity or interest.
rootOrigin: Middle English: from Old French reaume, from Latin regimen 'government'.
examples: It is not beyond the realm of possibility.
She is well-known in the realm of science.
memoryTrick: REALM: Think of a REAL KING overseeing his Kingdom.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'relm'.

definition: Strange or odd; unusual.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Latin peculiaris 'of private property', from peculium 'property'.
examples: There is a peculiar smell in this room.
He has a peculiar way of walking.
memoryTrick: PECULIAR: A person who keeps PEcking at their CULinary dishes is odd or strange.
commonMistake: Confusing peculiar with particular.

definition: Required by a legal, moral, or other rule; compulsory.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Latin obligatorius, from obligare.
examples: Attendance is obligatory for all staff.
It is obligatory to wear a seatbelt.
memoryTrick: OBLIGATORY: You are OBLIGED (forced) to do it.
commonMistake: Mispronouncing the stress; it's ob-LIG-atory, not ob-li-GAY-tory.

definition: Done, given, or acting of one's own free will.
rootOrigin: Late Middle English: from Latin voluntarius, from voluntas 'will'.
examples: The organization is funded by voluntary contributions.
He took voluntary redundancy.
memoryTrick: VOLUNTARY: Doing something as a VOLUNTEER.
commonMistake: Confusing voluntary (by choice) with volitional (the cognitive act of willing).

definition: Relating to the use of one's will.
rootOrigin: Mid 17th century: from Latin volitio, from volo 'I wish'.
examples: Eating is a volitional act, whereas digesting is not.
She acted with full volitional awareness.
memoryTrick: VOLITIONAL: Contains VOLITION, which sounds like WILL-ITION.
commonMistake: Using it interchangeably with voluntary in all contexts; volitional is more technical/psychological.

definition: Conferred as an honor, without the usual requirements or functions.
rootOrigin: Mid 17th century: from Latin honorarius, from honor.
examples: He received an honorary doctorate from the university.
She is the honorary president of the charity.
memoryTrick: HONORARY: Given out of HONOR, not for money.
commonMistake: Misspelling as 'honourary' (UK generally spells honour, but honorary without the 'u').

definition: The ability to understand and share the feelings of another.
examples: She had great empathy for her patients.
Empathy is essential in counseling.
memoryTrick: Empathy is 'Enter Path' - entering the path of another's feelings.
commonMistake: Confusing empathy (sharing feelings) with sympathy (feeling pity).

definition: Feelings of pity and sorrow for someone else's misfortune.
examples: They had great sympathy for the flood victims.
memoryTrick: Sympathy is 'Sorry' - feeling sorry for someone.
commonMistake: Confusing it with empathy (actually feeling what they feel).

definition: In a joyous or cheerful way.
examples: The children gleefully opened their presents.
memoryTrick: Glee sounds like 'gee', an expression of excitement.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'glefuly'.

definition: To disorder or disarrange (someone's hair), typically by running one's hands through it; to upset the calmness of.
examples: He ruffled the boy's hair affectionately.
Try not to let his comments ruffle you.
memoryTrick: Ruffle sounds like 'rough', making something rough and messy.
commonMistake: Using it only for physical objects; it can also mean to annoy someone.

definition: An item of clothing.
examples: She wore a long, flowing garment.
memoryTrick: Garment starts with G, like Gear to wear.
commonMistake: Using it for everyday casual speech instead of 'clothes'.

definition: Making a high-pitched piercing cry or sound.
examples: The children were shrieking with delight.
memoryTrick: Shriek has 'sh' which usually means quiet, but here it's the exact opposite—loud!
commonMistake: Confusing shriek with shrink.

definition: Having a gently calming effect.
examples: She listened to soothing music to help her sleep.
memoryTrick: Soothing makes things 'smooth'.
commonMistake: Spelling it with one 'o' (sothing).

definition: To walk or move in a leisurely, casual, or aimless way.
examples: We decided to wander around the old town.
memoryTrick: Wander implies walking without wondering where to go.
commonMistake: Confusing wander (walk) with wonder (think).

definition: The evaluation or estimation of the nature, quality, or ability of someone or something.
rootOrigin: Latin 'assidere' (to sit by, typically as an assessor)
examples: A proper assessment of the situation is necessary.
memoryTrick: Assessment assesses (tests) your skills.
commonMistake: Forgetting double 's' in the spelling.

definition: Make or cause to make the slightest movement.
examples: The door was stuck and wouldn't budge.
memoryTrick: If you push a heavy badge, it won't budge.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'budget'.

definition: A person's or animal's nature, especially as it permanently affects their behavior.
examples: He had a very gentle temperament.
memoryTrick: Temperament relates to someone's 'temper' or constant mood.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'temperment' without the middle 'a'.

definition: Not affected by alcohol; not drunk. Also: serious, sensible, and solemn.
examples: He remained sober throughout the party.
A sober assessment of the facts.
memoryTrick: Sober starts with S, like Serious.
commonMistake: Forgetting its secondary meaning (serious/quiet).

definition: Without interruption; constantly.
examples: She talked incessantly about her new job.
memoryTrick: In (not) + cease (stop) -> never stopping.
commonMistake: Spelling it with one 's' (incesantly).

definition: Not loud because of being obstructed in some way; muted.
examples: I could hear muffled voices from the next room.
memoryTrick: If you wear a muffler (scarf) over your mouth, your voice is muffled.
commonMistake: Using it for visual obstruction instead of sound.

definition: Done in a hurry, or moving with urgent haste.
examples: She felt rushed into making a decision.
memoryTrick: Rushed rhymes with crushed—if you rush, you might crush something.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'rusted'.

definition: Separate or cause to separate into curds or lumps (spoil/sour).
examples: The hot weather caused the milk to curdle.
A scream that made my blood curdle.
memoryTrick: Curdles makes curds.
commonMistake: Confusing 'curdle' with 'cuddle'.

definition: Of similar character.
examples: His feeling was akin to panic.
memoryTrick: A + kin = like a relative (similar).
commonMistake: Using 'akin with' instead of 'akin to'.

definition: Give special importance or prominence to (something) in speaking or writing.
examples: The teacher emphasized the need for daily practice.
memoryTrick: Emphasize adds emphasis (weight) to words.
commonMistake: Adding 'on' after it (e.g., 'emphasize on' is wrong; just 'emphasize something').

definition: A concentrated artillery bombardment over a wide area; a concentrated outpouring of questions or blows.
examples: The speaker faced a barrage of hostile questions.
memoryTrick: Barrage sounds like barrel—firing many barrels at once.
commonMistake: Mispronouncing the end (it sounds like 'garage').

definition: Expressed in terms intended to persuade or impress; related to the art of speaking or writing effectively.
examples: He asked a rhetorical question that required no answer.
memoryTrick: Rhetorical relates to an orator (speaker).
commonMistake: Using 'rhetorical question' to mean any unanswered question, rather than one intended to make a point.

definition: Situated or occurring nearer to the end of something than to the beginning; denoting the second or second mentioned of two people or things.
examples: Of the two choices, I prefer the latter.
memoryTrick: Latter sounds like Later. The latter comes later in the sentence.
commonMistake: Confusing 'latter' with 'later'.

definition: Causing annoyance or stimulating a reaction, emotion, or action.
examples: His tone was highly provoking.
It was a thought-provoking documentary.
memoryTrick: To 'poke' someone is provoking.
commonMistake: Confusing provoking with providing.

definition: A short journey undertaken in order to deliver or collect something, especially on someone else's behalf.
examples: I have to run a few errands before I come home.
memoryTrick: You Run An (eR-rANd) to get things done.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'error'.

definition: A light ringing sound such as that made by metal objects being shaken together.
examples: She heard the jingle of his keys.
memoryTrick: Jingle bells, jingle bells... (easy connection to sound).
commonMistake: Using it to describe heavy sounds.

definition: Shut (a door, window, or lid) forcefully and loudly.
examples: Please don't slam the door!
memoryTrick: Slam goes BAM!
commonMistake: Confusing slam with slump.

definition: A dull, heavy sound, such as that made by an object falling to the ground.
examples: The heavy book hit the floor with a thud.
memoryTrick: Thud sounds like the heavy drop it describes (onomatopoeia).
commonMistake: Confusing thud with dud.

definition: Crying noisily, making loud, convulsive gasps.
examples: She broke down, sobbing into her hands.
memoryTrick: Sob is a sad sounding word for sad crying.
commonMistake: Spelling it with one 'b' (sobing).

definition: A burial ground; a graveyard.
examples: They visited his grave at the cemetery.
memoryTrick: Cemetery has three e's—Eeeee (scary ghost sound in a graveyard).
commonMistake: Spelling it with an 'a' (cemetary).

definition: Go or move back or further away from a previous position.
examples: The floodwaters slowly began to recede.
memoryTrick: Re (back) + cede (go). Go back.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'receed'.

definition: Continue firmly or obstinately in an opinion or a course of action in spite of difficulty, opposition, or failure.
examples: If symptoms persist, consult a doctor.
memoryTrick: Persist is to insist on persevering.
commonMistake: Using it with 'to' instead of 'in' (e.g., 'persisted to talk' instead of 'persisted in talking').

definition: A person who attends a funeral as a relative or friend of the dead person.
examples: Mourners gathered around the grave.
memoryTrick: A mourner goes to mourning in the morning.
commonMistake: Confusing mourn with morning.

definition: Moves away from the land; recedes (used of the tide). Also means to gradually lessen or reduce.
examples: His courage began to ebb.
memoryTrick: Ebb sounds like 'end' (the water comes to an end).
commonMistake: Often used without its partner 'flow' (ebb and flow).

definition: A deep or considered thought about something; the act of thinking deeply.
examples: He sat in quiet rumination.
memoryTrick: Ruminate like a room mate—you spend a lot of time thinking about them.
commonMistake: Using it for quick thoughts instead of deep, obsessive pondering.

definition: Speak to someone or about something in a scornfully derisive or mocking way (मज़ाक उड़ाना).
examples: They scoffed at his attempts to apologize.
memoryTrick: If you scoff, you 'cough' at someone's idea out of disrespect.
commonMistake: Confusing it with scold.

definition: Deprived of a close relation or friend through their death.
examples: The bereaved family was offered support.
memoryTrick: Bereaved = Be + robbed. You feel robbed of a loved one.
commonMistake: Using it for general sadness instead of grief from death.

definition: Emotionally disturbing or distressing.
examples: Going through a war is a highly traumatic experience.
memoryTrick: Trauma = trouble that leaves a mark.
commonMistake: Overusing the word for minor inconveniences.

definition: Happen or occur afterward or as a result.
examples: When the alarm goes off, panic ensues.
memoryTrick: Ensue = In + sequence (happens next).
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'ensure'.

definition: Make less severe, serious, or painful.
examples: Drainage schemes have helped to mitigate this problem.
memoryTrick: Mitigate makes it mild.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'militate'.

definition: A postmortem examination to discover the cause of death or the extent of disease.
examples: The autopsy revealed that he died of poison.
memoryTrick: Auto (self) + opsis (sight) = seeing the inside yourself (medical exam).
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'autopsey'.

definition: A person living in solitude as a religious discipline; someone who lives alone and shuns human companionship.
examples: He became a hermit after his wife died.
memoryTrick: Hermit sounds like 'here me sit' alone.
commonMistake: Misusing it for someone who just stays in on a weekend.

definition: Very great in amount; having such a great effect on you that you feel confused and do not know how to react.
examples: The urge to sleep was overwhelming.
memoryTrick: Over + whelming (crushing wave) = too much to handle.
commonMistake: Using 'overwhelming' when something is just slightly difficult.

definition: Strong, healthy, and full of energy.
examples: We went for a vigorous walk in the hills.
memoryTrick: Vigorous sounds like rigorous—both take energy.
commonMistake: Spelling it with 'ou' (vigourous), though British English allows vigour, vigorous has no 'u'.

definition: An improvement in the condition or strength of something.
examples: The city is seeing a revival in its theater scene.
memoryTrick: Re (again) + vive (live) = to live again.
commonMistake: Confusing it with survival.

definition: The universe seen as a well-ordered whole.
examples: He wondered about our place in the cosmos.
memoryTrick: Cosmos sounds like cost-most (the universe is the biggest thing).
commonMistake: Using it interchangeably with just 'space' when it implies the ordered universe.

definition: The quality or state of being conscious or aware of something; a mental state achieved by focusing one's awareness on the present moment.
examples: Mindfulness can help reduce anxiety and stress.
memoryTrick: Your mind is 'full' of the present moment.
commonMistake: Confusing it with just 'thinking a lot'.

definition: Living or occurring at the same time; belonging to or occurring in the present.
examples: She loves reading contemporary literature.
memoryTrick: Con (with) + tempo (time) = with the current time.
commonMistake: Misspelling with an 'a' instead of 'o' (contempary).

definition: With great interest or enthusiasm; vigorously pursuing something.
examples: She read the news avidly every morning.
memoryTrick: Avid = A Very Interested Dude.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'avoid' just based on spelling.

definition: Give the impression or sensation of being something or having a particular quality (प्रतीत).
examples: It seemed like a good idea at the time.
memoryTrick: Seem sounds like 'see'—it's how things are seen.
commonMistake: Using 'seemed like' instead of 'seemed to be' improperly.

definition: Attempt to find (something).
examples: You must seek permission before entering.
memoryTrick: Seek rhymes with peek. You peek to seek.
commonMistake: Using 'seeked' instead of the irregular past tense 'sought'.

definition: Existing or occurring at or on the surface; exterior; not thorough, deep, or complete.
examples: He had only a superficial knowledge of the subject.
memoryTrick: Super (above) + facial (face) = just on the surface of the face.
commonMistake: Misusing it to describe something great (like 'super').

definition: Deal effectively with something difficult.
examples: She has a lot of strategies to cope with stress.
memoryTrick: Cope = Can Overcome Problems Effectively.
commonMistake: Saying 'cope up with' instead of just 'cope with'.

definition: A collection of weapons and military equipment; an array of resources available for a certain purpose.
examples: The country has a large nuclear arsenal.
memoryTrick: Arsenal sounds like 'arson all'—a place with weapons that could burn it all.
commonMistake: Thinking it only applies to literal guns.

definition: The capacity to recover quickly from difficulties; toughness.
examples: His resilience after the accident was inspiring.
memoryTrick: Resilience = Re + silence the critics by bouncing back.
commonMistake: Spelling it with 'ce' instead of 'ce' (resiliance).

definition: Seeming about to happen and causing worry; emerging or becoming visible as a large, threatening shape.
rootOrigin: Probably Low German or Dutch origin
examples: There is a looming deadline for the project.
memoryTrick: Looming sounds like 'zooming'—it's zooming towards you.
commonMistake: Using it for positive events.

definition: The persistent inability to believe that one's success is deserved or has been legitimately achieved as a result of one's own efforts or skills (feeling fake/depression).
examples: Even after winning the award, she struggled with imposter syndrome.
memoryTrick: Imposter = fake. Syndrome = condition. Feeling like a fake condition.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'impostor syndrom'.

definition: Occurring or extending between events; occurring between things.
examples: Much had changed in the intervening years.
memoryTrick: Inter (between) + vene (come). Coming between.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'interfering'.

definition: Open to more than one interpretation; ambiguous or undecided, especially in a way that is intended to deceive or mislead.
context: A politician giving a deliberately vague answer.
examples: The professor's remarks were so equivocal that students left the lecture more confused than enlightened.
memoryTrick: Equi (equal) + vocal (voice). Giving equal voice to both sides to avoid committing to one — hence ambiguous.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'equivalent' (equal in value).

definition: Intended for or likely to be understood by only a small number of people with a specialized knowledge or interest.
examples: The conference featured papers on esoteric aspects of quantum geometry that baffled all but a handful of attendees.
memoryTrick: Esoteric sounds like 'isolated territory' — knowledge kept in a small, isolated group.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'exotic' (originating in a foreign country).

definition: Given to sudden and unaccountable changes of mood or behavior.
context: Unstable, unpredictable weather patterns or human behavior.
examples: The administration of justice became capricious, dependent entirely on the whim of the ruling monarch.
memoryTrick: Capricious sounds like 'Capricorn' (the goat) — jumping around unpredictably from cliff to cliff.
commonMistake: Spelling it as 'capricous'.

definition: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way.
context: A silent, slow-acting poison or ideology.
examples: The pernicious influence of disinformation campaigns slowly eroded public trust in democratic institutions.
memoryTrick: Pernicious sounds like 'vicious' but with a 'p' — a vicious effect that creeps in slowly.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'precocious' (developing early).

definition: Showing great care, attention, and persistent effort.
context: A student preparing meticulously for a career-defining exam.
examples: Through assiduous research in the dusty archives, the historian uncovered the long-forgotten treaty.
memoryTrick: Assiduous contains 'ass' — work your ass off with constant effort.
commonMistake: Confusing it with 'acidic' (sour, containing acid).

definition: Tending to talk a great deal; extremely talkative.
context: An energetic speaker who dominates the conversation.
examples: Usually loquacious, the normally chatty host was rendered completely speechless by the surprise announcement.
memoryTrick: Loquacious shares the root 'loqu' (like soliloquy or eloquent) which means to speak. Someone loquacious speaks a lot.
commonMistake: Confusing with 'lugubrious' (looking or feeling sad).

definition: Showing a lack of courage or determination; timid or cowardly.
context: An advisor refusing to speak truth to power out of fear.
